Company basics

Founded in 1857 and headquartered in Saint Louis, Missouri, Spire Inc. is a publicly traded regulated natural gas utility company serving Alabama, Mississippi, and Missouri.

It operates approximately 63,000 miles of pipelines, providing utility services to 1.7 million homes and businesses in these states.

The quite recent "big thing" about the company was the announced in July 2025 acquisition of Piedmont Natural Gas Tennessee business from Duke Energy Corporation (DUK) and the associated expansion to a new state.

The acquisition of the $2.48 billion enterprise value on a cash-free, debt-free basis, subject to customary closing adjustments, is on track to close in the First Quarter of 2026. It will increase Spire's pipeline mileage by over 7,000 and the customers by more than 200,000.

Company Performance

This is how the general market has evaluated the common stock of the company, SR, for the past 10 years:

We'll compare the total return performance for the past ten years of Spire Inc. to that of the broader market, iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM), iShares U.S. Utilities ETF (IDU) as a representative for the US utilities, and to the Tortoise North American Pipeline Index ETF (TPYP), as a specific representative for the average North American gas pipeline company:

The comparison shows that Spire is lagging behind all these ETFs. While SR has managed only recently to surpass its pre-COVID total return values, the ETFs we observe have done so in a much faster manner, nowadays standing at two or more times the 10-year total return of Spire.

The expected future performance of Spire Inc. is more on the cautious side, according to the majority of the SA Analysts and the Quant rating, giving the company the neutral "Hold" score. The Wall Street analysts are more optimistic regarding the SR's future performance, with an average "Buy" rating.

The direct comparison to Gas Utilities shows that while Spire underperforms the National Fuel Gas Company (NFG) and the big dog in the sector - Atmos Energy Corporation (ATO), it performs in line with the average US-regulated Gas Utility:

Capital Structure

Below is Spire's capital structure as of the Third Quarter of 2025. The capital structure for the previous four quarters is also presented for comparison in the short term.

As of the end of September 2025, the total debt outstanding of Spire was $5.24 billion. SRJN will increase this value by $200 million to a total of $5.44 billion. The company has $242 million in preferred equity, junior to the debt, and at the lowest position in the capital structure is the common equity of approximately $3.15 billion. We can calculate a Debt-to-Equity ratio of 1.6, above the average of 1.36 for the regulated US gas utilities, as of January 2026.

Dividends

SR has an impressive dividend history of 36 consecutive years of uninterrupted distributions, the last 22 of them of dividend growth.

SR's TTM dividend amounts to $3.18, and with the last closing common share price of $84.06, the stock's distribution yield is 3.78%. This is what the dividend yield has looked like for the past 10 years:

The forward estimations for SR's distribution and dividend yield look like this:

Spire Inc. has approximately 59 million common shares outstanding. With a projected forward annual distribution of $3.30 on each, we can calculate $194.7 million in annual dividends for common shares. The company had annual TTM total interest expenses of approximately $204 million, and the new issue will increase this number by $12.8 million to approximately $221 million. On an annual basis, the common shares' distribution is enough to cover 88% of the expected total interest expenses.

Credit Rating

This is the title of the rating action with which S&P affirms the 'BBB+' credit rating of Spire inc., but also decreases the Outlook for it to "Negative", with respect to the aforementioned acquisition of Piedmont Natural Gas Tennessee business from Duke Energy Corporation. Here are S&P's reasons that triggered the Outlook revision:

The negative outlooks on Spire, Spire Missouri, and Spire Alabama reflect the possibility that consolidated FFO to debt could weaken to consistently below our 12% downgrade threshold. Our base case assumes the company’s FFO to debt weakens to the 11%-12% range following Spire’s acquisition of the Tennessee local distribution assets.

We could lower our ratings on Spire, Spire Missouri, and Spire Alabama over the next 18 months if Spire maintains consolidated FFO to debt of below 12%. This could occur if anticipated growth from the acquisition does not materialize, or the company funds the acquisition with a greater-than-anticipated level of debt.

Spire's OTC bonds

The image above shows how Spire's senior debt issues are currently priced. As SRJN is a long-term obligation of the company, we'll compare its yield to the 5.67% YTW of the longest-term OTC senior bond (of a subsidiary) - the '51 issue, rated A1 by Moody's. Above, we calculated a YTM of 6.56% for SRJN. Being five notches lower in credit quality than the '51 senior note, I'd say the 90 basis points higher yield for SRJN makes it overvalued to the senior debt of comparable duration.

Here, we can make another consideration, too. The '51 senior debt issue is priced with approximately 85 basis points above the US treasuries with the longest duration:

This credit spread is above the average of 60 basis points for the US corporate-issued Single-A debt issue:

Investors are requiring a higher credit spread for the senior debt of Spire, compared with the average peers of the same credit quality, indicating a cautious approach. Not only is SRJN overvalued to the senior debt of the company, but the senior debt itself is indicating a weakness. Potential investors should consider this when making a decision.

Peers comparison

The Gas Utilities sector does not have other listed debt issues, so I'll use all the priced below par term Utilities baby bonds for the peers comparison.

The securities with their metrics:

The comparison shows that SRJN is priced very close to two other Utilities baby bond additions, which IPOs we have recently covered - DTK and XELLL. I personally am more interested in the discounted Utilities baby bonds, which offer similar YTM, but better appreciation potential on a downshift in the long-term treasury yields. It is worth mentioning Entergy's baby bonds ELC, EMP, and EAI, offering considerably better credit quality at 40-50 basis points lower YTM.

Use of Proceeds

We estimate that we will receive net proceeds of approximately $193.5 million from the sale of the Notes in this offering after deducting the underwriting discount and estimated offering expenses payable by us. We estimate that we will receive net proceeds of approximately $222.5 million if the underwriters exercise in full their over-allotment option to purchase up to an additional $30.0 million in principal amount of the Notes. We intend to use such net proceeds, along with other funds, to redeem all of the outstanding shares of our Series A Preferred Stock with an aggregate $250.0 million liquidation preference, upon which the corresponding 10,000,000 outstanding depositary shares representing our Series A Preferred Stock will also be redeemed, and for other general corporate purposes. For the avoidance of doubt, nothing contained in this prospectus supplement constitutes a notice of redemption of our Series A Preferred Stock or such depositary shares.

Source: SRJN Prospectus

Now, this is an interesting moment. Spire is issuing a 6.375% junior subordinated debt to redeem a lower-positioned security in the capital structure, with a lower coupon.

Apparently, the reason for this is that the preferred stock dividends are not tax-deductible, unlike the interest on the junior subordinated notes, which will reduce the issuer's taxable income. Since this was also true when SR-A was issued, one has to wonder why the preferred stock was issued in the first place.

Optional Redemptions

We may redeem the Notes at our option before their maturity: (i) in whole or in part, on one or more occasions at any time on or after March 1, 2031, at a redemption price equal to 100% of the principal amount of the Notes being redeemed plus accrued and unpaid interest; (ii) in whole, but not in part, at any time before March 1, 2031, at a redemption price equal to 100% of their principal amount plus accrued and unpaid interest thereon, if certain changes in tax laws, regulations or interpretations occur; or (iii) in whole, but not in part, at any time before March 1, 2031, at a redemption price equal to 102% of their principal amount plus accrued and unpaid interest thereon, if a rating agency makes certain changes in the equity credit criteria for securities such as the Notes.

Source: SRJN Prospectus

Addition to the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF

The face value outstanding of the new exchange-traded baby bond SRJN of $200 million makes it a potential addition to the ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred & Hybrid Securities Index during some of its upcoming rebalancing. If that happens, it will also be included in the holdings of the main benchmark iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF). PFF is an exchange-traded fund that seeks to track the investment results of the above-mentioned index and is rather important for us due to its influence on the behavior of many of the exchange-traded securities.

Conclusion

SRJN is a borderline investment-grade baby bond. The recently downgraded outlook of Spire's credit score heed for caution when making investment decisions about the company's debt. A downgrade of the company's credit score will drive its junior subordinated debt into the high-yield territory. At this point, SRJN looks overvalued to Spire's senior long-term debt, and unappealing compared to Utilities' baby bond peers.
